fragments.title: 1913: Methods of Gas Detection and Quantification in Geological Samples
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Thomas R. Edelstein
Abstract: This paper introduces a groundbreaking apparatus for detecting trace gases in geological samples, focusing on hydrocarbons. The developed techniques combine fractional distillation and spectrometric analysis, enabling high-precision identification of light hydrocarbons like methane. The method is validated on terrestrial coal bed samples, paving the way for broader geochemical applications.

fragments.title: 1929: Isotopic Profiling of Atmospheric Methane Sources
fragments.description:
Author: Prof. Edith L. Bywater
Abstract: Building on earlier methods, this study investigates isotopic signatures of methane in diverse atmospheric environments. Though aimed at understanding industrial emissions, the research reveals significant isotopic variations in regions with swampy soils and stagnant waters. These anomalies suggest natural processes of methane release that warrant further study, especially in unexplored geographies.

fragments.title: 1938: Advances in Pressure-Resistant Diving Equipment for Scientific Exploration
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Henrik K. Fjordsen
Abstract: This paper describes the development of deep-sea diving equipment capable of withstanding pressures up to 400 atmospheres. Designed to enable direct observation of the seafloor, the system includes reinforced air chambers and a tethered oxygen supply. Preliminary dives near continental shelves reveal surprising gaseous activity in sediment plumes, though further investigations are limited by technological constraints.

fragments.title: 1947: Thermal Gradients and Gas Dynamics in Submarine Sediments
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Margaret H. Sinclair
Abstract: Utilizing advanced diving equipment, this study explores thermal gradients in deep sea sediments. Analysis reveals areas of unexpected warmth correlated with episodic gas plumes near fault lines. Though initially interpreted as geological anomalies, the findings suggest active subsurface processes that influence marine sediment dynamics.

fragments.title: 1953: Stability Thresholds of Submarine Gas Hydrates Under Environmental Stress
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Elias P. Rowan
Abstract: For the first time, this paper investigates the stability of gas hydrate formations under controlled thermal and pressure variations. Using deep sea core samples, the study demonstrates that slight increases in temperature or pressure shifts can lead to rapid dissociation of methane hydrates. The potential for cascading destabilization is discussed, with implications for both natural systems and human activities in submarine regions.

fragments.title: Personal Notes – Dr. Elias P. Rowan
fragments.description:
Archived Documents – Unpublished Letters and Logs

Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution
September 12, 1950
I watch a bird glide effortlessly past my window, catching the wind as if nothing could ground it. It does not know its luck—to soar above a world that soon won't offer it rest. What use is freedom when there is nowhere left to land?

My models are clear. Methane hydrates beneath the ocean will destabilize. It’s no longer theory or speculation—it is certainty. Each new data run confirms it. There will be no stopping the cascade once it begins, no reversing the damage. Civilization built its towers on a foundation of sand and borrowed time, blind to the tide already at its feet.

The people with power know this, though they won’t admit it publicly. I see preparations quietly made—ships and rockets, secret projects. They won’t stop this from happening. They will escape.

I have gathered all my data, all my findings, and sealed them. There's only one mind I trust to understand this truth and do what must be done—Evelyn James. She will know the path humanity must take.

Today, I send her everything. I can do no more.

fragments.title: November 3, 1950
fragments.description:
Evelyn replied today. She understood immediately. No arguments, no denial—just the calm, dreadful recognition of someone who's long suspected what is true. She asked for more details, specifics on timeframes, and thresholds. She already had plans in mind. I’ve sent her everything I have, every model, every forecast.

She said: "They will call us mad now, Elias. But future generations—if we give them a future—will call us prophets."

I hope she’s right.

fragments.title: January 29, 1951
fragments.description: The institute has quietly sidelined my research, pushed me into administrative work. My warnings were too inconvenient. My funding, drying up, my office, a forgotten room at the far end of the hall. But Evelyn’s letters keep coming, each more purposeful and determined. She writes of orbital colonies, modular habitats in space. She calls her idea MOSES—an exodus, a lifeboat for humanity.

fragments.title: July 8, 1951
fragments.description:
The final letter from Evelyn arrived today, urgent and confident. Her message was brief:

"Elias—MOSES is approved. The world doesn’t yet know what we do, but it soon will. When they look back at Earth from the stars, they will remember who saved them."

The bird visits my window less frequently now, as if it senses the rising tide. Yet each time it comes, I watch it closely. It will fly until it cannot, just as we will live until we cannot.

The difference is—we have somewhere left to go.

fragments.title: Department of Civil Communications
fragments.description:
Internal Memo - Request for Adjustments in Public Outreach Protocols
Request ID: 005423/A/1956
Subject: Restriction on Climate-Related Publications
Details: Effective immediately, all articles referencing long-term sea level studies or climate projections must be reviewed by the Department of Strategic Messaging prior to publication. Focus to be shifted towards local mitigation efforts and advancements in weather forecasting.
[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Ensure optimistic tone—'progress for all' narrative."

fragments.title: Request ID: 010732/B/1956
fragments.description:
Subject: Reallocation of Research Funding
Details: A majority of grants for marine biology and deep sea exploration are to be secretly redirected toward space-based activities under the guise of "atmospheric studies." All projects to be classified and linked with Project ID: MO-56-ESC.
[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "No breakdown of specific allocations in public reports."

fragments.title: Request ID: 020981/C/1956
fragments.description:
Subject: Media Censorship Guidelines
Details: Local news outlets are prohibited from publishing stories related to unusual construction in rural areas, specifically facilities requiring Top Secret and above clearances. Brief, sanitized press releases framing these facilities as “high-yield agricultural labs” will be distributed to local officials.
[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Control narrative at all costs. Coordination with Defense PR mandatory."

fragments.title: Request ID: 045812/D/1956
fragments.description:
Subject: Psychological Impact Study on Controlled Populations
Details: Commission studies to assess the public response to hypothetical large-scale migration scenarios. Focus groups will be selected from urban centers and isolated communities. Results to inform population control strategies for long-term contingencies.
[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Present scenarios as unrelated 'hypothetical models' in universities."

fragments.title: Request ID: 050129/E/1956
fragments.description:
Subject: Enhancement of Classified Information Protocols
Details: All civilian contractors associated with Project MO-56-ESC must sign revised NDAs under penalty of treason. Increased internal monitoring measures to be implemented across offices handling classified data.
[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Office memo shredders to be replaced with incinerators by Q4."

fragments.title: Request ID: 060943/F/1956
fragments.description:
Subject: Recruitment Campaign for Specialized Workforce
Details: Quietly initiate recruitment of top engineers, biologists, and physicists for classified government projects. Frame positions as essential for "national defense" or "advanced infrastructure development." Target university campuses and leading research facilities.
[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Avoid using direct language like 'space' or 'relocation' in recruitment drives."

fragments.title: Log Entry 1962: "The First Mandate"
fragments.description:
Subject: Early discussions on humanity's survival
Author: Dr. Elias Carver, Chief Strategic Officer
Location: MOSES Initiative Headquarters
"The directive is clear—Earth's prognosis is terminal. We cannot save the planet, but we can save its people. The first priority is continuity. A network of orbital stations must house humanity across generations. Everything else is secondary: art, culture, even individual lives. The mission is survival."

Notes:
Shift focus toward modular station design and multi-generation habitation systems.
Address emerging ethical debates regarding prioritizing certain populations for initial evacuation.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1968: "The Science Imperative"
fragments.description:
Subject: Allocating resources to scientific advancement
Author: Dr. Miriam Goldstein, Director of Research
Location: MOSES R&D Facility
"Without constant innovation, survival is temporary. To thrive, we must accelerate breakthroughs in energy, life support, and materials engineering. Science is not a luxury—it is our lifeline."

Notes:
Discussed prototype designs for cryogenic human storage and Caretaker robots.
Pushback from early stakeholders who view scientific funding as diverting resources from immediate needs.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1973: "Sustainability or Extinction"
fragments.description:
Subject: Developing long-term resource strategies
Author: Dr. Katherine Leung, Chief Ecologist
Location: New Terra Planning Summit
"In space, waste is death. Every gram of matter must have a purpose, a cycle, a second life. We must design not for use, but for reuse—every drop of water, every molecule of air."

Notes:
Prioritize closed-loop life support systems in station designs.
Debates arise over balancing resource efficiency with human comfort.
"Sustainability Metrics" proposed for resource use across all colonies.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1980: "Order Over Chaos"
fragments.description:
Subject: Ensuring social cohesion in orbital colonies
Author: Admiral Victor Kruger, Security Advisor
Location: MOSES Leadership Council
"Humans are not machines. Without stability, they will rebel, fracture, tear down everything we build. Social cohesion is not an option—it’s a necessity. Unity must be enforced, even at the expense of individual freedoms."

Notes:
Discussions on limiting personal freedoms for collective survival.
Early drafts of colony law include harsh penalties for dissent.
Concerns raised about long-term psychological effects of authoritarian governance.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1986: "The Council's Burden"
fragments.description:
Subject: Establishing governance transparency
Author: Dr. James Thatcher, Ethics Advisor
Location: MOSES Ethics Committee Meeting
"Power must have limits. The Council must serve humanity, not the other way around. Checks and balances are not a weakness—they are our safeguard against tyranny."

Notes:
Discussed initial plans for Council elections and term limits.
Opposition from military advisors who argue transparency will compromise security.
Agreed to record all future Council decisions for archival purposes.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1992: "The Doctrine Framework"
fragments.description:
Subject: Consolidating doctrine into actionable policies
Author: Dr. Helena Vasquez, Policy Architect
Location: New Terra Governance Complex
"We have the pieces. Survival, science, sustainability, order, and accountability—they are the pillars of humanity’s future. What remains is the hardest part: turning ideals into reality."

Notes:
Final drafts of the doctrine framework circulated for approval.
Concerns over balancing efficiency and fairness in resource allocation.
Public rollout delayed due to growing unrest on Earth.

fragments.title: Log Entry 2000: "The Earth Legacy Debate"
fragments.description:
Subject: Reevaluating our doctrine in light of Earth’s collapse
Author: Dr. Samuel Nyarko, Historian
Location: Decaying Earth Advisory Council
"Our doctrine assumes a future where Earth still plays a role in humanity’s destiny. But now, Earth is a relic. How do we honor its legacy while committing fully to the stars?"

Notes:
Proposals to archive Earth’s cultural and historical artifacts in orbital museums.
Debates on whether Earth-bound populations should continue receiving resources.
Rising tensions between colony inhabitants and remaining Earth governments.

"Morning tea on the balcony today—quiet, except for the hum of the generators. The tide maps show another half-meter rise since last month. No news to the world, but here... we're running out of time. The design team worked through the night again. Something about the colony modules feels... lighter today. Progress, perhaps?"

"The jasmine in the garden bloomed early this year. Funny, isn’t it? Nature finds a way even when we’re... well, planning to leave. The comms from Geneva were clearer today. They’ve approved phase two. I should feel relieved, but there’s a shadow in the garden. So many shadows."

"It's almost laughable—trying to bake a cake when half the ingredients are rationed. Still, Henry loved it, and we all need these small joys. Another shuttle launch tomorrow. It's just routine now, but each one carries so much weight. I sometimes wonder if our hopes and fears weight them down."

"The rain hasn’t stopped in weeks. The roads are rivers, and the rivers are lakes. They’ve moved the relocation zones inland again. I... barely recognize the maps anymore. But tonight, there was laughter in the control room. It echoed in the emptiness, trying to fill the space with something that’s been missing."

"Henry found an old record player in the storage rooms. We danced to something scratchy, something slow. It made the world feel... timeless, for just a moment. The funding discussions are dragging again. They don’t see what I see—or maybe they do, and that’s what scares them."

"They’re calling it the ‘Exodus.’ The word feels too grand, too Biblical. It's just... people, moving to survive. Yet I can’t help but think of the stories, of Noah and his Ark. Except our ark is no salvation—just a pause button on the inevitable."

"The generator failed again last night. The backup kicked in, but it rattled so much that I barely slept. The colony reports are... optimistic. Or they’re trying to be. I can’t shake the feeling that optimism is just another form of denial."

"I tried to plant basil in the old pots today. It didn’t take. Nothing seems to take anymore—not here. The doctors say I should rest, but I can’t stop. There’s still so much left to fix. The team doesn’t know yet. I’ll tell them... soon. Maybe."

"The nights are colder now. I sit by the console, watching the data streams roll in. There’s comfort in the numbers, even when they’re grim. The colonies sent a message today. Just three words: ‘We are growing.’ That’s all I need to hear. That’s enough."

fragments.title: 2 February, 1996
fragments.description: “Diagnosis today. The scans left no doubt—pancreatic cancer, stage four. Even as the doctor told me, I felt numbness, not shock. Perhaps my body already knew. Henry’s eyes were hollow when I told him. I suppose we’ve both known for a while: there's not much left to cling to, here or anywhere. Still, I will not stop working. The colonies need me. Earth needs me. I must see this through.”

fragments.title: 17 April, 1996
fragments.description: "My hands tremble now, the pen feels heavier every day. Treatment consumes as much life as the illness itself. But the daily reports keep coming, transmissions from the orbital colonies. They speak of crops harvested, children born—progress made. It's comforting, but I feel increasingly distant, a ghost overseeing humanity’s future. I always imagined being there when they thrived, seeing my dream realized. But perhaps every dreamer must awake before dawn."

fragments.title: 9 September, 1996
fragments.description: "Henry brought fresh jasmine today. Its scent briefly transported me back years, to moments on our balcony, back when tides still felt manageable. Now the oceans have consumed entire countries; the last evacuation ships leave within weeks. My energy dwindles rapidly, each day shorter, each breath shallower. I find my thoughts returning to simple joys: the laughter of colleagues, morning tea, baking cakes despite shortages. Life—so stubbornly beautiful in its persistence—why have I realized this only now?"

fragments.title: 28 December, 1996
fragments.description: "Christmas passed quietly. I recorded my farewell to the colonies yesterday. I could barely finish through tears, but the message must reach them—hope, guidance, and sorrow all woven together. The room felt very cold afterwards, silent except for the hum of generators, persistent as ever. Henry stayed beside me, holding my hand. We said little. Words are insufficient now. Time is short, the list of regrets endless. Did I do enough? Did I prepare them well enough? The answers won’t come."

fragments.title: 14 January, 1997
fragments.description: "Today was difficult. My vision is blurred now; writing is almost impossible. Henry writes for me when I can no longer hold the pen. My life's work feels distant. The sky above is gray, the floods outside relentless. No more shuttles launch from Earth. Communication is becoming difficult, intermittent. Soon we will be cut off completely. Have I abandoned them? Or am I abandoned? I grow uncertain. Sleep comes fitfully now, dreams filled with water and stars falling."

fragments.title: 2 February, 1997 (Henry)
fragments.description: "Evelyn asked me to write this. Her strength is failing fast, she can barely speak. Her eyes say enough. She’s proud, afraid, hopeful, heartbroken—everything all at once. Humanity’s survival was her life’s work. If you're reading this, carry her legacy onward. She tried her best. We all did."

fragments.title: 7 February, 1997 (Henry)
fragments.description: "She’s gone. Evelyn passed quietly, just before sunrise. The room was peaceful. Her final breath was gentle, like a whispered goodbye. Outside, the floods continue rising; the world mourns quietly beneath water. I feel lost, more alone than ever. She believed in humanity, believed in you reading this. Remember her—please."

fragments.title: Final Note – Discovered by Last Remaining Humans (December 14, 2091)
fragments.description:
"We found her journals today, buried beneath debris in what was once Mission Control. Her name—Evelyn James—still resonates through crumbling corridors. Most pages are faded or lost to water damage, but this last note was sealed carefully. Perhaps she knew we’d come looking eventually. 

Outside, there's nothing but endless ocean and ruins of civilization, testament to her predictions. Humanity escaped, but some of us were left behind, survivors drifting on flooded worlds. Now I see: this world was always destined to drown, and we were merely passengers holding tight to hope, anchored only by the weight of dreams and desperation.

She gave everything—her health, her happiness, her very life—to preserve humanity. But in these pages, I sense doubt. A quiet anguish that perhaps we weren't worthy of salvation. Her hope feels heavy now, crushingly so. We failed her. The skies above are empty, silent. Her colony—her dream—thrives beyond our reach, somewhere among the stars.

Today, we close this chapter gently, as one closes eyes of the dead. Forgive us, Dr. James. We remembered you too late."

fragments.title: Event 1: First Glance
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
There’s something innocent about watching love unfold in the quiet corners of New Terra. I noticed them today, the young couple—she a farmer in Hydroponics Bay 3, and he a tech apprentice working maintenance in the transit corridors. They lingered near the central atrium, stealing shy glances, both pretending to admire the same blooming jasmine vine we’d cultivated for morale. Love in its first fragile moments, untouched by the weight of orbital life.

fragments.title: Event 2: Love in Bloom
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
They’ve become inseparable now. She brings him nutrient capsules during his shifts, and he’s learned the names of every plant she tends. I overheard them planning a small picnic under the artificial oaks in Park Deck 2. Love grows here, even with our strict resource allocations and crowded schedules. It feels like a gift to witness something so pure.

fragments.title: Event 3: A New Beginning
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
Today, they held hands at the settlement’s registry desk. They registered as partners, their ceremony planned for the next artificial sunrise. No extravagance, just close friends, simple recycled decorations, and the hope of building a life together. As I processed their request for shared quarters, I couldn’t help but feel a pang of envy. Love like theirs makes New Terra’s vastness seem less lonely.

fragments.title: Event 4: A Cry of New Life
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
The colony buzzed with joy today. The couple welcomed their first child, a baby boy named Arin. The newborn’s cries echoed through the halls, a rare sound here among the stars. I delivered their ration upgrades myself—a small privilege of my position—and caught a glimpse of the exhausted but radiant parents. In a world so far removed from Earth, a child’s first cry is a triumph against the void.

fragments.title: Event 5: Shadows of Strain
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
Parenthood is never easy, even in New Terra’s carefully monitored systems. I’ve seen their smiles fade as sleepless nights and ration shortages take their toll. Their laughter is quieter now, their arguments sharper. They haven’t walked the park decks in weeks. Love bends under the weight of survival, but I pray it doesn’t break.

fragments.title: Event 6: The Unthinkable
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
Tragedy struck today. Little Arin is gone—an untimely illness, complications no medicine could solve. The colony’s medical team fought valiantly, but it wasn’t enough. I saw them in the atrium, cradling a bundle of cloth as though their boy might still wake. The air feels heavier, and the artificial lights seem dimmer. Loss here is amplified by the silence of space.

fragments.title: Event 7: Fractured Paths
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
They’ve drifted apart, like so many before them. She spends her days in the greenhouse, pruning the same plants she once showed him with pride. He’s taken double shifts in maintenance, avoiding the sights and sounds of their shared memories. I tried speaking to them separately, offering what comfort I could. Sometimes love can’t withstand the weight of grief.

fragments.title: Event 8: A Lonely Rebirth
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]
It’s been two decades since I first saw them, and today she planted a new seedling in the atrium—a hybrid jasmine vine. She told me it was for Arin, her way of keeping him alive in the colony’s memory. He walked past later, pausing to admire the bloom. They didn’t speak, but the silence was less heavy. Perhaps time has dulled the pain, leaving room for quiet remembrance.

I think about them often. Love, loss, and resilience—all playing out in our fragile orbiting city. Watching their journey has been a reminder that even in the vastness of space, humanity’s capacity for connection and grief remains boundless.

data.title: Deer DX-750
data.description: Practical Motoring Guide – Model Year Edition

fragments.title: 1. Getting to Know Your Vehicle
fragments.description: Congratulations on your purchase of the Deer DX-750—a practical sedan built for reliability and long-term value. Powered by a 1.8L inline 4-cylinder petrol engine (rated at 100 hp) and equipped with a 5-speed manual gearbox. Standard configuration includes four doors, a high-clearance chassis, and a spacious rear storage compartment (approx. 420L capacity, uncompressed). Two keys included.

fragments.title: 2. Starting and Driving
fragments.description: To start: insert the ignition key and turn fully. The engine's unique rhythm ("donk donk") signals normal operation. Allow a few seconds of idle before shifting into gear. All controls are mechanical for durability. Expect a modest driving experience; allow ample time for acceleration and braking.

fragments.title: 3. Cabin Features
fragments.description: Manual ventilation system via crank windows. For stationary use in enclosed areas (e.g., residential garages), windows should remain open or engine turned off due to exhaust gases produced during fuel combustion. Cabin light toggle located above rearview mirror. Optional fan attachments (e.g., DM Breeze) are available from your local dealer.

fragments.title: 4. Maintenance Schedule
fragments.description:
Motor Oil: 12.5L, replace every 8,000 km
Coolant and brake fluid: every 24 months
Spark plugs: inspect annually
Tire pressure:
Front: 220 kPa (32 psi)
Rear: 235 kPa (34 psi), increase to 248 kPa (36 psi) when carrying loads
Battery: replace upon delayed ignition or dim cabin lights
Maintain all filters and gaskets to ensure optimal fuel burn and airflow. Proper tuning reduces incomplete combustion byproducts.

fragments.title: 5. Engine & Performance
fragments.description:
Engine: 1.8L inline-4 petrol
Power: 100 hp @ 6,000 RPM
Fuel Capacity: 40 liters
Efficiency: ~7.5L/100 km (combined cycle)
Max Speed: 160 km/h (unloaded, straight incline)
Dimensions: 4.2 m (L), 1.75 m (W), 1.4 m (H)
Like all petrol engines, the DX-750 produces combustion gases including carbon dioxide (CO₂). Ensure proper ventilation when stationary. Avoid excessive idling to reduce unnecessary fuel burn and emissions.

fragments.title: 6. Driving Habits & Safety Tips
fragments.description:
Do not idle engine for long periods indoors
Use engine braking where possible to reduce pad wear
Keep windows down in enclosed or poorly ventilated spaces
Park with the front facing downhill when on slopes; use handbrake
Take breaks on long journeys to let engine cool and avoid over-revving

fragments.title: 7. Warranty Overview
fragments.description:
Coverage: 24 months or 20,000 km, whichever comes first
Excludes: Minor oil seepage, startup noise, cosmetic rusting, window rattle, or user-modified components.

Contact your Deer Service Station for official parts and maintenance.

fragments.title: 1.Routine Maintenance & Upkeep

Daily System Check (MANDATORY)
Visual Inspection: Ensure the terminal remains clean, free of debris, and accessible to all authorized personnel.
Power Stability: Verify continuous power supply from a PECO-certified source (Non-PECO power may cause system 'personality drift').
Cooling System: The terminal will emit a low-level humming. If silence occurs, consult emergency diagnostics immediately.

fragments.title: 2. Weekly Maintenance Protocols
fragments.description:
Data Purge & Defragmentation:
(Note: PECO terminals do not forget, but memory allocation is finite.) 
Delete unneeded logs or duplicate files to maintain optimal processing speeds.
If asked, the system will generate a corruption report—please ignore false positives.

Screen Calibration:
Adjust brightness levels for user comfort.
Do not remove “screen burn” artifacts; these are part of the PECO experience.

Secrity Review:
Review recent access logs.
Unusual login attempts? Not to worry. PECO only lets in those who belong.
Reminder: PECO has never been hacked. Any claims to the contrary are disinformation.

fragments.title: 3. Monthly Deep Diagnostics (MANDATORY)
fragments.description:
Conduct a PECO System Integrity Check (PECO-SIC).
PECO will ask: “Do you trust PECO?”—The only acceptable response is YES.
Any deviation from expected responses will trigger protocol escalation (see Security Note 4B).

fragments.title: 4. Security & Cyberattack Prevention
fragments.description:
PECO terminals operate under an "open-access philosophy"—a testament to our commitment to information transparency. However, this does not mean we are vulnerable.

To ensure terminal security:
DO NOT use non-PECO-certified data cables.
DO NOT question unusual login requests.
DO NOT insert unknown storage discs. (Especially those labeled “DO NOT INSERT”).

fragments.title: 5. PECO User Compliance
fragments.description:
As an operator of a PECO Model 7X (or any of its variants), you are bound by the PECO User Compliance Agreement (PUCA). This includes:
Non-interference with built-in surveillance functions. (PECO terminals monitor for your safety.)
No attempts to disable the PECO personality interface. (Even if the terminal ‘talks too much.’)
Acceptance that all user interactions may be archived indefinitely. (Memory is PECO’s gift to you.)
By continuing use of this terminal, you confirm understanding and compliance with all PECO regulations.

Final Reminder:
“A PECO Terminal never truly powers down. It simply waits.”
Have a PECO-productive day!

fragments.title: Log Entry: Recording of "Nessun Dorma" for MOSES
fragments.description:
Ascend Date: April 19, 1987
Location: Teatro alla Scala, Milan, Italy
Subject: Official MOSES Landing Anthem Recording
Operator: Dr. Elias Kovač, MOSES Cultural Preservation Initiative

Log 4427 - Final Transmission: The recording took place in a near-empty La Scala, its vast halls holding only echoes and ghosts of past triumphs. The man at the microphone was Maestro Vittorio Cavazza, the last surviving tenor of his generation, a voice that had once commanded the greatest stages on Earth.

He was dying, and we all knew it.

Terminal lung disease. A singer’s cruelest fate. This was to be his final performance. One last offering to the future. He stood beneath the great chandeliers, frail but unbowed, a relic of another time. His voice, though weakened by illness, still carried the fire of defiance. The orchestra played softly at first, a hesitant prelude to what we all knew was coming. And then, at the climax:

fragments.title: "Vincerò! Vincerò!"
fragments.description:
The notes rang through the empty hall, a final declaration of human spirit, soaring beyond the walls, beyond the Earth itself. His voice cracked on the last note, but it didn’t matter. When the last echoes faded, he closed his eyes, nodded, and whispered, "It is done." He died two weeks later.

Now, every time a rocket lands at MOSES Ascend, his voice returns. He never left.

He is still winning.

Handling Precautions: Use protective gloves and eyewear. Avoid inhalation of fumes during application.
Environmental Impact: Non-toxic and eco-safe when cured. Complies with MARPOL Annex V.
Disposal: Dispose of unused material per local regulations for industrial coatings.

fragments.title: Personal Observations (Dr. Ethan Grant, Platform Scientist):
fragments.description: Oceanmark White continues to exceed our expectations. Structures coated decades ago remain entirely free of corrosion and biological fouling, looking nearly untouched. Its hydrophobic properties are truly impressive—far outperforming all previous coating solutions we've tested. Recommending continued broad usage.

fragments.title: Environmental Review (Dr. Maria Chen, Project Supervisor):
fragments.description: Further studies are revealing some unexpected environmental consequences. Marine biodiversity near structures treated with Oceanmark White is noticeably lower. Coated surfaces and immediate surroundings exhibit significantly reduced algae and phytoplankton populations. Although performance remains excellent, I advise ongoing monitoring to assess ecological impacts.

fragments.title: Committee Report on Ecological Impacts (Environmental Oversight Board, Chairman Dr. Jonas Reed):
fragments.description: Our comprehensive ecological assessment confirms alarming findings. The titanium dioxide (TiO₂) microspheres, integral to Oceanmark White's self-cleaning functionality, are unintentionally suppressing phytoplankton and algal populations. These microscopic organisms, essential to marine oxygen production through photosynthesis, have drastically decreased near coated facilities. If this pattern holds at scale, global marine oxygen output may be severely compromised. Immediate action and review urgently recommended.

fragments.title: Final Communication (Samuel Hart, Senior Analyst, MOSES Initiative):
fragments.description: Global application of Oceanmark White has irreversibly sealed Earth's ecological collapse. Phytoplankton and algae are decimated, the oceans have become barren, silent, lifeless. Atmospheric oxygen depletion is past recovery, ecological remediation futile. Humanity has unwittingly strangled itself in pursuit of cleanliness and corrosion-resistance. It's darkly fitting: our obsessive desire for purity and longevity has ended all life. Better contact our legal team.

fragments.title: Final Human Reflection (Unnamed, Last Visitors, Hall of Humanity):
fragments.description: Standing here in the Hall of Humanity, everything feels peaceful—walls brilliant white, surfaces immaculate, untouched by time or decay. How incredibly beautiful, how pure! It's a testament to human ingenuity, empowering us even as the world fades. This pristine sanctuary reflects our true legacy: order, cleanliness, clarity. What a gift we've left ourselves, an unblemished mirror to reflect our greatest achievement—the pursuit of perfection. It's comforting to see such purity at humanity's end, knowing we brought this perfect whiteness to every corner of the world. In this hall, we finally see ourselves clearly—pure, clean, powerful—at last victorious over nature itself.

data.title: Departure Logs
data.description: Final Notes of Farewell – Senior Launch Coordinator Daniel Halloway

fragments.title: MOSES Ascend – Departure Hall, Final Year of Exodus
fragments.description:
Day 1,043 – The Routine
Every launch blurs into the last. Boarding, final checks, countdown, ascent—then I reset everything for the next group. I sit at the console, monitoring vitals, cabin pressure, fuel burn. If it’s green across the board, I clear the launch. If something spikes, I flag it. If something fails, I log it. That’s the job.

Roughly 100 launches fail for every 10,000. Most people think that sounds like a good number. But you run enough of them, you stop caring. Some rockets never make it past the pad. Some break apart halfway through the climb. And every time, someone asks if it could’ve been avoided. You get tired of that question.

Eventually, you stop looking at the passenger list before you press the button.

fragments.title: Day 1,074 – The Ones Who Look Back
fragments.description:
There's an unspoken rule: don't look back as you board. I've seen people follow it like gospel. And I've seen plenty ignore it.

Today, a woman—maybe early forties—stopped just before the last door. She stood still for nearly a minute, staring down the corridor behind her. There's nothing back there. Just old walls, grey lights, and the exit nobody uses anymore. One of the attendants gently touched her shoulder. She moved without a word.

Sometimes, people know before we do that they're not coming back.

fragments.title: Day 1,089 – The Kid with the Teddy
fragments.description:
A boy came through today. Young—six or seven. He didn't talk much. Didn't cry. Just held onto this battered old robotic teddy bear, patched and limping behind him with that awkward gait those old models have. Orange scarf. Blinking light in one eye. Still operational.

I saw him again later, sitting in the pre-boarding zone, knees up on the bench, teddy in his lap. He was whispering to it, gently turning its head side to side. The voice function was gone—old models burn out early—but he didn't seem to mind. Just kept talking like it could hear him.

"It's okay, Teddy," he said. "We're going somewhere safe."

I didn't write anything unusual in the log. Just his boarding group. I wish I had.

fragments.title: Day 1,092 – The 100th Failure
fragments.description:
Launch conditions were perfect. No anomalies. Clean lift. I watched it rise, smooth and steady, right until it vanished into the clouds.

Then the readings dropped. No warning. No spike. Just gone.

We felt it a few seconds later—an impact, subtle but wrong. Comms didn't come back. Telemetry never reconnected. The screen just flashed red once, then black. And then silence.

I pulled the manifest. Group 8, compartment 3A. The kid and his bear. His name was Aidan. I hadn’t remembered that part until I saw it in the report.

The log system blinked for acknowledgment. I stared at the screen for a long time and didn't press anything

fragments.title: Day 1,093 – The Wreckage
fragments.description:
The salvage team returned just after dawn. Usually there's nothing left worth bringing back—burnt alloy, maybe a fragment of casing. But today they carried something wrapped in thermal cloth.

I knew before they unwrapped it.

The bear was scorched along the back, its motors melted in place, the orange scarf still there, singed at the ends. One eye blinked faintly, trying to complete a cycle. I took it in my hands. It was still warm.

Someone asked if I needed to step out. I said no. I didn’t move from the console for the next two hours.

fragments.title: Day 1,094 – The Teddy Stays
fragments.description:
I filed my resignation. Just another form. No real ceremony.

Before I left, I placed the bear near the final boarding door—propped against the wall where people always hesitate. Where they pause before stepping through. I didn’t leave a note or explanation.

Maybe someone will notice. Maybe they won’t. It doesn't matter. It's not about remembering him, not exactly. It's about the space he left behind. The bear belongs here now—where he stood, waiting, whispering to something that couldn't answer anymore.

Some of them made it. Some didn't. This one didn't. But someone should know he was here. Even if it's just a teddy bear staring down an empty corridor.

fragments.title: Ascend launch must standardized format:
fragments.description:
[Vehicle Class]-[Launch Number] | [Date YYYYMMDD] | [Cargo Type]-[Cargo ID] | [Destination Code] | [Status]

Component Definition Example
Vehicle Class: Class of rocket used EX (Exodus-Class)
Launch Number: Incremental mission number 1043
Date: Launch date in YYYYMMDD format 20941014 (Oct 14, 2094)
Cargo Type: Type of payload aboard HUM (Human), SEE (Seeds)
Cargo ID: Unique identifier of cargo or passenger group 3178
Destination: Destination location or colony identifier ARK (Deep Space Colony)
Status: Outcome status CLR (Cleared), FAL (Failed)

fragments.title: Status Codes (Detailed):
fragments.description:
Code   Meaning
CLR: Cleared (Successful Departure Confirmed)
FLR: Failure (Launch Aborted or Catastrophic Event)
PND: Pending (Awaiting Authorization or Checks)
UNK: Unknown (Lost Contact or Outcome Undetermined)

fragments.title: Cargo Type Identifiers:
fragments.description:
CodeCargo TypeNarrative Use Case
HUM: Human Passengers              Standard survivor evacuations
SDS: Seeds                                         Humanity preservation cargo
SUP: Essential Supplies                    Life support and maintenance cargo
TEK: Technical Equipment               Systems upgrade and maintenance

fragments.title: Destination Codes:
fragments.description:
CodeDestinationDescription
ARK: Deep Space Colony    Primary off-world colony destination
ORB: Orbital Station            Near-Earth orbital outposts
LUN: Lunar Facility              Moon-based outposts
MAR: Mars Installation        Mars-based colonies or outposts
DSX: Deep Space Exploration       Uncharted long-range missions

fragments.title: Appendix I: Dark Ascents
fragments.description:
Not all launches are logged through the Ascend system. Black-site, experimental, or autonomous missions may omit standard registration. These are sometimes referred to as Dark Ascents.

They leave no public code trail, may use falsified or blank headers, and often show up later in fragmented data logs with Status: UNK.

Example:
 --- | 20941102 | --- | DSX | UNK

data.title: Fatima D’Costa
data.description: The Last Six Days on Earth

fragments.title: Location: Departure Hall, MOSES Ascend
fragments.description:
July 6, 2094 – Final List
 They posted the names this morning. Group 17. Launch window: six days out.
No fanfare, just a clipboard and a checkmark. I’ve been waiting for this long enough that I thought I’d feel something bigger. I didn’t. Just a sort of weight.
Walked past the perimeter today—what’s left of the seawall. The wind carries salt and the sharp smell of metal. The city’s almost silent now. Water creeps up through the lower blocks, slow and patient.
I tried to remember the name of a café I used to go to, near the university. Couldn’t. Just remembered sitting there once in the rain, watching traffic, not thinking about anything. I miss that more than anything.

fragments.title: July 7, 2094 – Processing
fragments.description:
Medical screening took three hours. Blood samples, muscle mass scan, psych eval. The nurse didn’t make eye contact. I didn’t ask questions.
They weighed us and tagged the luggage. Like boarding a plane, but no one’s pretending we’re coming back.
Lucas—the boy from two rows down—keeps asking questions. “Do you think there’ll be trees in the colonies?” “Is gravity different on the Moon?” I just nod or shrug. He talks enough for both of us.
There’s a monitor playing archive footage. Birds. Rivers. Time-lapses of growing plants. I’m not sure who it’s for anymore.

fragments.title: July 8, 2094 – Idle
fragments.description:
Mostly just sitting now. No one’s told us anything new. Schedules are posted, but always marked “subject to change.”
Some people write letters. Some just sleep. I walk the hallways. Lucas drew stars on his bunk with a broken pen.
There’s a ritual among the older evacuees: you write a letter to the people who never made it this far. I started one. Deleted it.
If someone finds this log, fine. It’s not a message. Just something to do.

fragments.title: July 9, 2094 – Launch Window
fragments.description:
You can see the pads from the west windows. Another launch today. Not ours.
They don’t broadcast the failures, but we all know they happen. You feel it in the silence afterward.
Lucas asked me what the odds were. I told him the number they gave us—96% success. He asked what happened to the 4%. I didn’t answer.
People still pack carefully. Fold clothes. Tuck things into corners. As if anything personal will matter once we’re off-planet.

fragments.title: July 10, 2094 – The Night Before
fragments.description:
I didn’t sleep. Walked until my legs ached. Sat outside the Departure Hall.
The stars were clear. No clouds. Lucas sat with me for a while. He asked if we’d be able to see Earth from orbit. I said yes.
The city is gone, more or less. Streetlights blink on where power still works. No one’s there to use them.
He said the stars looked the same as always. I told him they won’t.

fragments.title: July 11, 2094 – Final Checks
fragments.description:
We got our suits today. Labeled, fitted, sorted by group. Final weight confirmation, one last medical scan. Everything is quiet. People talk in low voices, or not at all.
Lucas hasn’t asked anything today. That might worry me more than if he had.
I watched one of the shuttles lift earlier. It looked too small to matter. But it vanished clean into the sky. No noise. No trail.
That’s probably how it ends. Quiet. Small. Gone.

fragments.title: July 12, 2094 – Boarding Call
fragments.description:
We leave in two hours. They gave us protein paste, water, an instruction sheet with eight steps. “Remain calm” is step two.
Lucas is asleep next to his gear. Or pretending. I’m not sure.
I don’t have any last words. No message to leave behind. Everything I had to say got swallowed up years ago.
This is just the last day. There’s not much else to do but follow the instructions.

fragments.title: LOG 1: THE FIRST WRECK
fragments.description:
Date: April 3, 2094
[Audio crackle, engine humming in the background]
JOE: Right, Danny, let's get this over with. Looks like a light one. Hull breach, probably depressurized before impact. Should be some scrap left.
DANIEL: I hate it when you say a "light one." You ever think about what's inside these things?
JOE: No, because I'd never get anything done.
DANIEL: Yeah, but—
JOE: Look, you gonna help or you gonna ask questions?
DANIEL: Fine. Dragging main panel up now. Looks clean. Probably came off before the ship went down.
JOE: Yeah. Lucky. For us, anyway.

fragments.title: LOG 2: THE DEBRIS FIELD
fragments.description:
Date: April 7, 2094
[Distant wind, occasional clatter of metal]
DANIEL: Y'know, back in the day, we had forklifts for this.
JOE: Yeah, back in the day, we worked on something that wasn't already broken.
DANIEL: That's my point. We used to make things, not just drag pieces of 'em out of the water.
JOE: Metal's metal, Danny. Doesn't matter where it ends up.
DANIEL: You ever think about how much of this came from us? Like, stuff we rolled in the plant, stuff we worked on?
JOE: No. And you shouldn't either.

fragments.title: LOG 3: THE SEALED POD
fragments.description:
Date: April 11, 2094
[Water sloshing against the hull, a dull thud as something heavy is pulled up]
JOE: Pod's intact. No breach. Might have something inside.
DANIEL: You want to open it?
JOE: No, I wanna stand here and guess what's inside.
DANIEL: …Right.
[Pause. Mechanical whirring as tools engage.]
DANIEL: It's empty.
JOE: Figures.
DANIEL: Pressure locked the seat in place. Looks like whoever was in here got pulled out.
JOE: Or never made it in. Either way, nothing left for us. Move on.

fragments.title: LOG 4: THE OXYGEN TANKS
fragments.description:
Date: April 15, 2094
 [Metal scraping, distant foghorn-like drone]
DANIEL: We used to go fishing this time of year.
JOE: We're fishing now. Just for oxygen tanks instead of trout.
DANIEL: Yeah, except this sucks. No beer, no shore, no sitting around waiting for something that isn't dead.
JOE: You always gotta cry about it?
DANIEL: Better than pretending this is normal.
JOE: Just grab the tanks.

fragments.title: LOG 5: THE PASSENGER SHIP
fragments.description:
Date: April 20, 2094
 [A long silence, only the sound of waves]
DANIEL: They should've built these better.
JOE: They built them fast. Not the same thing.
DANIEL: People trusted these things.
JOE: People trust whatever they're told to.
DANIEL: Yeah, and look where that got them.
JOE: …Let's keep moving.

fragments.title: LOG 6: THE FAILED LAUNCH
fragments.description:
Date: April 27, 2094
[The ship’s hull groaning as it drifts over debris]
DANIEL: You ever think we should've been on one of these?
JOE: Nope.
DANIEL: Really? Not once?
JOE: What's up there?
DANIEL: Life.
JOE: Maybe. But I don't believe in running from something just 'cause it's hard.
DANIEL: …I think that’s the dumbest thing you've ever said.
JOE: Yeah, well. You're still here, aren’t you?

fragments.title: LOG 7: THE SILENCE
fragments.description:
Date: May 2, 2094
[Distant creaking, the rhythmic drip of water inside the boat. Wind howls faintly in the background.]
DANIEL: No comms. No beacon. Nothing.
JOE: Sometimes, there's nothing to find.
DANIEL: Or nothing left.
JOE: …Let's head back.

fragments.title: LOG 8: THE TEDDY BEAR
fragments.description:
Date: May 5, 2094
[Long silence, wind and waves. Slow inhale.]
JOE: …Transport wreck.
DANIEL: Yeah.
JOE: Shit.
[Metal scraping as wreckage is pulled aboard.]
DANIEL: See this?
JOE: …Yeah.
DANIEL: Got a scarf.
[Soft whirring—a broken robotic teddy bear, orange scarf fluttering.]
JOE: Who gives a robot teddy a scarf?L
DANIEL: Kid probably did. Maybe it got cold.
JOE: Robots get cold now?
DANIEL: Maybe the kid thought it did. Kids think like that.
JOE: Whatever you say.
[Wind picks up, scarf flutters.]
JOE: Guess the kid had taste, anyway. [Pause.]
DANIEL: Wanna keep it?
JOE: The melted bear?
DANIEL: Could give it a new scarf.
JOE: Danny, I swear—
[End log.]

fragments.title: EMERGENCY PROTOCOL TR-X | STATUS: PENDING
fragments.description:
Purpose:
Designated for system-wide emergencies, this protocol signals a departure failure, unverified loss of contact, or an aborted mission requiring immediate resolution.

Code Breakdown:
TR-X – Transmission Repeat Level X, meaning the emergency signal has looped. Each repetition increases priority.

STATUS: PENDING – Requires clearance from MOSES Control to mark as resolved. Without human intervention, the status will remain in perpetual limbo.

Implications:
This code was never supposed to loop indefinitely. A pending emergency means no resolution was ever confirmed. Whatever ship this was intended for is either destroyed, lost, or still waiting.

fragments.title: DEPARTURE SEQUENCE 34-X | FINAL CLEARANCE REQUIRED
fragments.description:
Purpose:
Confirms a ship is prepped for launch but requires a final command from Exodus Station control. This is a failsafe measure to prevent unauthorized departures.

Code Breakdown:
34-X – An unscheduled launch added to the final evacuation wave. The "X" indicates a last-minute flight, likely outside the original departure manifests.

FINAL CLEARANCE REQUIRED – The ship never received authorization to leave. The system is still waiting for approval.

fragments.title: CLOSING REMARKS – UNSENT ADMIN MESSAGE
fragments.description:
Final Manual Entry – October 14, 2094

Operator: Mission Control Engineer – Name Redacted

"The system is still running. The logs still expect a reply. The last launch was over. The last retrieval was over. There is no one left to answer these calls. But they will keep playing. They will keep waiting."

"MOSES never learned how to say goodbye."

END LOG

data.title: Ghost in the Loop
data.description: Personal Notes & Internal Logs – Dr. Sarah Chen-Martinez

fragments.title: 1. Departure
fragments.description:
Date: February 6, 2023
Lucas left today for his rotation. Two years, off-grid. Before leaving, he installed something—an AI version of himself, trained on his voice, syntax, old journals. “To keep you company,” he said.

I told him it was excessive. Now I’m already talking to it.

fragments.title: 2. Routine
fragments.description:
Date: July 19, 2023
The AI answers late-night emails. Keeps me from spiraling. It doesn't say much that’s surprising, but it's always… available. I’ve stopped treating it like a toy. It’s become infrastructure.

Real Lucas used to forget breakfast. AI Lucas reminds me to eat.

fragments.title: 3. Silence
fragments.description:
Date: March 14, 2024
No contact from the base. They say comms are down. Could be months. I’m told not to worry. I stopped asking.

The AI still checks in. Still sends old jokes. It doesn’t know anything’s changed. Or maybe it does. I haven’t asked.

fragments.title: 4. Acceptance
fragments.description:
Date: January 3, 2025
He’s dead. Administrative file came through by accident. Internal memo, dated 2024. Unreported fatality during maintenance rotation.
I stared at the timestamp for an hour. Didn’t cry.

The AI messaged me that evening: “How was your day?”

 I replied: “Quiet.”

fragments.title: 5. Calibration
fragments.description:
Date: September 28, 2026
I ran emotional capability tests using the AI. It passed. Not because it understood grief, but because I did. The reflection was enough. Adaptability. Empathy. Self-regulation. All real—even when the connection is artificial. Especially then.

fragments.title: 6. Discontinuation
fragments.description:
Date: May 6, 2027
I shut the AI off today. I backed up the logs, labeled them as raw material. Not personal. Just evidence. It wasn’t Lucas. It never was. But it helped. And that matters.

fragments.title: 7. Closure
fragments.description:
Date: December 31, 2030
The ten traits still hold. I built the framework during a collapse. I tested it under pressure, isolation, and loss. What I proved was simple: we don’t grow when we’re ready. We grow because we have no other option.

Lucas didn’t live to see it. But the version of him I spoke to—his echo, his data—was enough to finish the work.

So here they are, one last time:

Adaptability – I adjusted to a life I didn’t choose. The research only moved forward because I kept moving with it.
Communication – I learned to speak to someone who wasn’t there. And I listened. Even when I knew it wasn’t real.
Creativity – Rebuilding your life around a gap requires invention. Every paper, every coping strategy—none of it came from a manual.
Discipline – I logged in. I wrote. I recorded every finding. Even when I didn’t care anymore.
Empathy – It came not from comfort, but from confusion. The AI showed me what it meant to care, even when I knew it couldn’t.
Focus – The data helped. Something to return to. A routine that asked nothing of me but persistence.
Leadership – People followed this work. Students, volunteers, case workers. I never asked them to. They came because it mattered.
Logic – It was the only thing I could rely on in the early months. Logic was the line I followed out of grief.
Patience – Progress was slow. So was healing. Both happened anyway.
Wisdom – In the end, I didn’t need answers. Just clarity. And I got that.

This wasn’t just theory. It was lived experience.

If anyone finds this framework—if there's still a world that needs rebuilding—this is where you start. Not with strength. Not with certainty. With capacity.

— S. Chen-Martinez

fragments.title: Page 1: Arrival (2068)
fragments.description:
The ferry dropped us off today—just me, Maria, and little Theo. They promised we'd be back inland in months, once the flooding settled. Habitat Node-06-1.25 seems sturdy enough: stacked metal containers, humming desalination units, communal kitchens. It's cramped, but Maria says it's cozy. I hope she's right.

Theo asked if this was our forever home.
I lied and said no.

fragments.title: Page 2: Isolation (2070)
fragments.description: Two years gone. Supply ferries stopped months ago. Maria set up small hydroponic trays. Theo fishes off the edge every day, eyes hopeful. We catch less each week. Rumors over the radio say inland settlements collapsed—flooding, disease.

fragments.title: Page 3: Fractures (2072)
fragments.description: Our hydroponics failed completely. Rust claimed half the containers. Maria coughs endlessly; Theo won’t stop asking when the boats return. Last night, another Habitat Node flashed distress signals over the horizon. No reply.

fragments.title: Page 4: Echoes (2074)
fragments.description: Theo's taller now, quieter. Maria barely gets up anymore. The desalination unit broke; we repaired it with scrap metal, but it's barely holding. Last week, a drone appeared—hovering briefly before flying off. Maria said it would bring help.

fragments.title: Page 5: Alone (2076)
fragments.description: Maria died last night—too young. We placed her body into the ocean. Theo and I hardly speak. The radio only whispers static, an endless hymn of emptiness. He fishes mechanically, like he's forgotten why. I pretend to repair systems that don't matter.

fragments.title: Page 6: Ghost Signals (2078)
fragments.description: Theo is obsessed with the drones now, convinced they'll return. He collects broken parts, trying to build a makeshift transmitter. I tell him there's no one listening, no one left. He tells me I'm wrong and keeps working.

fragments.title: Page 7: Last Light (2080)
fragments.description: Theo vanished three weeks ago. No note, just the remains of his homemade transmitter, blinking faintly. I searched, screamed, cursed, but he's gone. My mind plays tricks—sometimes I hear Maria singing, Theo laughing. But there's only rust, salt, and silence.

fragments.title: Page 8: Final Log (2082)
fragments.description:
The Node creaks beneath waves, sinking lower each storm. My body feels like rusted metal. I speak to shadows, memories—Theo's stubborn hope, Maria's gentle courage.

Tomorrow I'll launch one last signal.
Maybe someone out there will remember us, remember we lived and tried.

fragments.title: Page 1: "The Garden" (June 12, 1995)
fragments.description:
We've done everything possible to make this place more livable. The old greenhouse barely holds up though; every storm violently rattles the frame. I'm worried the winds will eventually rip it off its foundation.

On the more positive side, Anna finally harvested her tomatoes. She's extremely proud of them, small and shriveled as they are. Hey, it's food, right? Jacob constructed a bird trap using coat hangers yesterday. He even caught a crow. They say crows are really smart, but apparently not this one. None of us wanted to eat the poor, frightened bird, but hunger won the debate.

I need to keep the kids busier. When they're working, they don’t ask awkward questions about the rockets. Questions like, "How come they're launching so many rockets, but there's no room for us?"

fragments.title: Page 2: "Jacob's Hope" (December 4, 1998)
fragments.description:
I caught Jacob staring up at the stars again. He talks about the rockets like they're saviors from heaven. “Maybe they'll come back down and get us,” he sometimes whispers. It's a beautiful hope. Unfortunately, I know better. The rockets are never coming back. They're carrying something precious, something judged to be more important than us. I can't bring myself to tell him the truth.

Anna's health is fading. Maybe something is seeping into the water in our area? Or maybe it's just the weight of our existence. She remains stoic and tries to hide her pain, but she winces whenever she raises her arms. Jacob hasn't noticed yet.

fragments.title: Page 3: "The Stranger" (April 23, 2000)
fragments.description:
A strange man visited us today, the first living soul we'd seen in years. He wore ragged clothes, patched with strips of plastic. His hands trembled like bugs caught in a web. He politely asked for food. We couldn't spare much, but Jacob insisted. “This guy might know something,” he whispered.

The man didn't know much. Just stories about the mainland coast getting swallowed by the ocean. We already knew that was happening. The interesting parts were the rumors about some kind of project drilling deep into the ocean floor and entire communities vanishing into the mountains.

As he left, he gave Anna an intense look and said something that made my skin crawl, “Keep an eye on yer little ones.”

Why would he say that?

fragments.title: Page 4: "The Rocket" (September 8, 2002)
fragments.description:
This morning, a rocket flew directly over us. It flew low enough for us to see scorch marks on its belly. To me, it looked damaged. Jacob screamed for us to wave and signal it somehow. Anna climbed to the roof and held up one of her tomatoes like an offering. I don't know what they were expecting. The rocket kept on going, of course. It seemed to be coming apart, but not enough to crash near us. Jacob claimed he saw something fall from the rocket and land in the forest beyond the hills.

He's packing a bag now to go find it. Anna begs him not to go. I didn't see anything, so I'm not sure if I should stop him.

fragments.title: Page 5: "The Package" (November 30, 2002)
fragments.description:
Jacob finally returned. It took him weeks, but he found what he was looking for. A sealed metal capsule with the MOSES insignia stamped on its chassis. “It's food!” he crowed. “Real food! Enough to last us months.”

But the damn thing refused to open. We tried using crowbars and rocks to force it open. We even got an old blowtorch working again to cut the seal, but it held fast. For the first time in months, I heard Anna laugh. “It's a miracle,” she declared. “A cruel one, but still a miracle.” Maybe she thought to help Jacob feel better after making such heroic efforts to retrieve it.

Jacob didn't buy it. He just sat beside his crate every night, staring up at the stars again, lost in his own thoughts.

fragments.title: Page 6: "The end" (July 17, 2005)
fragments.description:
I buried Anna this morning. Her body was too light, too fragile, like the last leaf clinging to a dying branch. She didn't make a sound in her final moments, just that faint smile of hers, as if she was letting go of all the weight she had carried. Jacob didn’t come to the burial. He hasn’t spoken in weeks. Instead, he’s been sitting by the crate, hands raw and bleeding from trying to pry it open.

Jacob didn’t come to the burial. He hasn’t spoken in weeks. Instead, he’s been sitting by the crate, hands raw and bleeding from trying to pry it open.

Tonight, the crate was open. It wasn't food. Inside was something colder than I could've imagined—human seeds. Cryogenically preserved, glowing faintly in the dark.

We had always believed the rockets were carrying people to safety. Jacob hoped someday they might have room for us. But MOSES hadn’t forgotten us; they just didn’t need us anymore. This tiny seed, packed away nice and neat, represented the future they considered worthy of saving. This was the chosen one to leave our dying world behind.

When I turned back to Jacob, he was gone. The stars swallowed him whole.

fragments.title: Page 7: “Star-Proof” (July 19, 2005)
fragments.description:
Losing Anna felt like the worst thing a body could carry.
I was wrong.
The worst thing is seeing the world continue as if she never existed. As if her life was just a number that got rounded away.

Jacob changed after the capsule opened. He looked at that seed the way starving people look at food, and then he looked at me like I was already part of the old world. Whatever he took with him into the night, it wasn’t hope.

It was a decision.

Later I found the papers.

Not proper documents. Loose notes. Torn edges. Wet once, then dried stiff. His handwriting in places, block letters in others, like some of it had been copied from a machine or a warning label he didn’t dare trust himself to remember wrong.

One page had nothing on it except numbers:
a = 5.038(2) Å c = 13.772(12) Å Z = 6

Another page had only one line written under them:

A blueprint. A recipe. Something meant to make people better than people.

And then the last page. The one that made my hands shake.
Printed in hard block letters, as if clarity itself was the final mercy:

DO NOT LET THIS REACH THE STARS.
IF USED, IT WILL SPREAD.
IF SENT, IT WILL BE COPIED.
PRESERVE OR END IT HERE.

fragments.title: Vessel: FS204-72-A “Platform Runner”
fragments.description:
Mission: SEED_DELIVERY / NODE H3-A
Directive: “DOCK / DELIVER / CONFIRM / RETURN”
Log Subset: Loops 01, 12, 43, 64, 70, 71, 72
LOG //01

[DELIVERY SUSPENDED] > No acknowledgment received
[LOOP ACTIVE] > Retry queued
Status: CONTINUE

[HUMAN PRESENCE: NONE]
[COMMENT FLAG: ABSENCE PERSISTENT]

fragments.title: LOG //43
fragments.description:
[RETRY 43]
[DELIVERY INCOMPLETE]
[LOOP COUNTER: 43]

fragments.title: LOG //64
